It varies from zero to four per cent by volume of the atmosphere and plays an important role in the weather phenomena. Water is present in the atmosphere in three... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: WATER IN THE ATMOSPHERE, EVAPORATION AND CONDENSATION, WATER IN THE ATMOSPHERE 87, WATER IN THE ATMOSPHERE 89. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Atmospheric Circulation and Weather Systems is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that wATER IN THE ATMOSPHERE Y ou have already learnt that the air contains water vapour.

The text explains that it varies from zero to four per cent by volume of the atmosphere and plays an important role in the weather phenomena.

The text explains that water is present in the atmosphere in three forms namely – gaseous, liquid and solid.

The text explains that the moisture in the atmosphere is derived from water bodies through evaporation and from plants through transpiration.

The text explains that thus, there is a continuous exchange of water between the atmosphere, the oceans and the continents through the processes of evaporation, transpiration, condensation and precipitation.

The text explains that water vapour present in the air is known as humidity.

The text explains that it is expressed quantitatively in different ways.

The text explains that the actual amount of the water vapour present in the atmosphere is known as the absolute humidity.

The text explains that it is the weight of water vapour per unit volume of air and is expressed in terms of grams per cubic metre.

The text explains that the ability of the air to hold water vapour depends entirely on its temperature.

The text explains that the absolute humidity differs from place to place on the surface of the earth.

The text explains that the percentage of moisture present in the atmosphere as compared to its full capacity at a given temperature is known as the relative humidity.

The text explains that with the change of air temperature, the capacity to retain moisture increases or decreases and the relative humidity is also affected.

The text explains that it is greater over the oceans and least over the continents.

The text explains that the air containing moisture to its full capacity at a given temperature is said to be saturated.

The text explains that it means that the air at the given temperature is incapable of holding any additional amount of moisture at that stage.

The text explains that the temperature at which saturation occurs in a given sample of air is known as dew point.

The text explains that evaporation is a process by which water is transformed from liquid to gaseous state.

The text explains that latent heat of vaporisation is the amount of heat energy required to convert a unit mass of a liquid into vapour without a change in temperature.

The text explains that increase in temperature increases water absorption and retention capacity of the given parcel of air.

The text explains that condensation also takes place when the moist air comes in contact with some colder object and it may also take place when the temperature is close to the dew point.

The text explains that condensation is influenced by the volume of air, temperature, pressure and humidity.

The text explains that wATER IN THE ATMOSPHERE 87 place: (i) when the temperature of the air is reduced to dew point with its volume remaining constant; (ii) when both the volume and the temperature are reduced; (iv) when moisture is added to the air through evaporation.

The text explains that however, the most favourable condition for condensation is the decrease in air temperature.

The text explains that forms of condensation can be classified on the basis of temperature and location.

The text explains that the ideal conditions for the formation of white frost are the same as those for the formation of dew, except that the air temperature must be at or below the freezing point.

The text explains that fog and Mist When the temperature of an air mass containing a large quantity of water vapour falls all of a sudden, condensation takes place within itself on fine dust particles.

The text explains that fog reduces horizontal visibility on earths’ surface to less than 1 km, while mist limits it to between 1 km and 2 km .

The text explains that as the clouds are formed at some height over the surface of the earth, they take various shapes.

The text explains that according to their height, expanse, density and transparency or opaqueness clouds are grouped under four types : (i) cirrus; (ii) cumulus; (iii) stratus; (iv) nimbus.

The text explains that these clouds are generally formed either due to loss of heat or the mixing of air masses with different temperatures.

The text explains that fUNDAMENTALS OF PHYSICAL GEOGRAPHY 88 of the earth.

The text explains that precipitation The process of continuous condensation in free air helps the condensed particles to grow in size.

The text explains that when the resistance of the air fails to hold them against the force of gravity, they fall on to the earth’s surface.

The text explains that the precipitation in the form of water is called rainfall, when the temperature is lower than the 00C, precipitation takes place in the form of fine flakes of snow and is called snowfall.

The text explains that when a layer of air with the temperature above freezing point overlies a subfreezing layer near the ground, precipitation takes place in the form of sleet.

The text explains that sometimes, drops of rain after being released by the clouds become solidified into small rounded solid pieces of ice and which reach the surface of the earth are called hailstones.

The text explains that it is very common in the equatorial regions and interior parts of the continents, particularly in the northern hemisphere.

The text explains that orographic Rain When the saturated air mass comes across a mountain, it is forced to ascend and as it rises, it expands; the temperature falls, and the moisture is condensed.

The text explains that after giving rain on the windward side, when these winds reach the other slope, they descend, and their temperature rises.

The text explains that cyclonic Rain You have already read about extra tropical cyclones and cyclonic rain in Chapter 9.

The text explains that please consult Chapter 9 to understand cyclonic rainfall.

The text explains that world Distribution of Rainfall Different places on the earth’s surface receive different amounts of rainfall in a year and that too in different seasons.

The text explains that the coastal areas of the world receive greater amounts of rainfall than the interior of the continents.

The text explains that the rainfall is more over the oceans than on the landmasses of the world because of being great sources of water.

The text explains that seasonal distribution of rainfall provides an important aspect to judge its effectiveness.

The text explains that in some regions rainfall is distributed evenly throughout the year such as in the equatorial belt and in the western parts of cool temperate regions.

The text explains that (i) Which one of the following is the most important constituent of the atmosphere for human beings?

The text explains that (a) Water vapour (c) Dust particle (b) Nitrogen (d) Oxygen

The text explains that fUNDAMENTALS OF PHYSICAL GEOGRAPHY 90 (ii) Which one of the following process is responsible for transforming liquid into vapour?

The text explains that (a) Condensation (c) Evaporation (b) Transpiration (d) Precipitation (iii) The air that contains moisture to its full capacity : (a) Relative humidity (c) Absolute humidity (b) Specific humidity (d) Saturated air (iv) Which one of the following is the highest cloud in the sky?

The text explains that (a) Cirrus (c) Nimbus (b) Stratus (d) Cumulus 2.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Fundamentals of Physical Geography, Chapter 10: Atmospheric Circulation and Weather Systems

revision_bullets: WATER IN THE ATMOSPHERE Y ou have already learnt that the air contains water vapour. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch10 pg 1)
It varies from zero to four per cent by volume of the atmosphere and plays an important role in the weather phenomena. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch10 pg 1)
Water is present in the atmosphere in three forms namely – gaseous, liquid and solid. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch10 pg 1)
The moisture in the atmosphere is derived from water bodies through evaporation and from plants through transpiration. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch10 pg 1)

Thus, there is a continuous exchange of water between the atmosphere, the oceans and the continents through the processes of evaporation, transpiration, condensation and precipitation. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch10 pg 1)
The actual amount of the water vapour present in the atmosphere is known as the absolute humidity. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch10 pg 1)
It is the weight of water vapour per unit volume of air and is expressed in terms of grams per cubic metre. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch10 pg 1)
The ability of the air to hold water vapour depends entirely on its temperature. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch10 pg 1)
The absolute humidity differs from place to place on the surface of the earth. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch10 pg 1)
The percentage of moisture present in the atmosphere as compared to its full capacity at a given temperature is known as the relative humidity. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch10 pg 1)

cases.name: Dew When
cases.point: Dew When the moisture is deposited in the form of water droplets on cooler surfaces of solid objects (rather than nuclei in air above the surface)... (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch10 pg 1)

cases.name: Frost Frost
cases.point: Frost Frost forms on cold surfaces when condensation takes place below freezing point (00C), i.e. the dew point is at or below the freezing point. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch10 pg 1)

cases.name: Fog
cases.point: Fog and Mist When the temperature of an air mass containing a large quantity of water vapour falls all of a sudden, condensation takes place within... (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch10 pg 1)

cases.name: Clouds Cloud
cases.point: Clouds Cloud is a mass of minute water droplets or tiny crystals of ice formed by the condensation of the water vapour in free air at... (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch10 pg 1)

cases.name: Cirrus Cirrus
cases.point: Cirrus Cirrus clouds are formed at high altitudes (8,000 - 12,000m). (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch10 pg 1)

Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
